strategy_research: {"strategies": [{"name": "Antonacci's Global Equity Momentum (GEM)", "description": "Three-way monthly rotation among US equities (SPY), international equities (EFA), and bonds (AGG) using a dual momentum filter: relative momentum picks the stronger equity market, absolute momentum (12-month return vs. T-bills) determines whether to be in equities at all.", "evidence": ["Gary Antonacci, 'Risk Premia Harvesting Through Dual Momentum' (SSRN, 2012)", "Antonacci, Dual Momentum Investing (McGraw-Hill, 2014) \u2014 USA Best Book Award, Personal Finance", "Jegadeesh & Titman (1993), 'Returns to Buying Winners and Selling Losers', Journal of Finance", "Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2012), 'Time Series Momentum', Journal of Financial Economics", "ReSolve Asset Management, 'Global Equity Momentum: A Craftsman's Perspective' \u2014 critical out-of-sample review"], "historical_sharpe": "0.87 (1974\u20132013 backtest); CAGR 17.43% vs. 8.85% for ACWI; max drawdown -22.7% vs. -60.2% for buy-and-hold", "market_conditions": "Works in trending bear markets (2000\u20132002, 2008) and sustained directional regimes. Fails in whipsaw markets, periods of simultaneous equity+bond drawdowns (2022), and during prolonged US equity dominance (2012\u2013present) where international allocation drags returns.", "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "AGG"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "low"}, {"name": "Keller's Vigilant Asset Allocation (VAA)", "description": "Aggressive breadth-momentum strategy using a front-weighted 13612W momentum score. If ANY offensive asset (SPY, EFA, EEM, AGG) has negative momentum, 100% of capital shifts to the highest-momentum defensive asset (LQD, IEF, or SHY). Combines crash protection with strong bull-market participation.", "evidence": ["Keller & Keuning, 'Breadth Momentum and Vigilant Asset Allocation' (SSRN 3002624, July 2017)", "Keller & Keuning, 'Breadth Momentum and the Canary Universe: Defensive Asset Allocation' (SSRN 3307823) \u2014 successor strategy addressing VAA's AGG sensitivity", "Allocate Smartly independent verification of VAA strategy returns"], "historical_sharpe": "0.94\u20131.10 (Dec 1970\u2013Dec 2016); CAGR 18.9%; max drawdown -13.0%; MAR ratio 1.46 vs. ~0.4 for 60/40", "market_conditions": "Excels in bear markets and volatile transitions. Weaknesses: AGG sensitivity causes false defensive signals (bond weakness alone triggers full exit even in equity bull markets); 100% concentration in a single ETF creates lumpy volatility; high turnover from front-weighted momentum formula.", "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "AGG", "TLT", "SHY"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "low"}, {"name": "Cross-Asset Momentum Ranking with Trend Filter", "description": "Rank a multi-asset universe (equities, bonds, gold, international) by a composite momentum score (e.g., 12-1 month return or 13612W). Hold only top-N assets that also pass an absolute trend filter (above 200-day MA or positive 12-month return). Combines cross-sectional and time-series momentum into one system.", "evidence": ["Asness, Moskowitz & Pedersen (2013), 'Value and Momentum Everywhere', Journal of Finance", "Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2012), 'Time Series Momentum', Journal of Financial Economics", "CME Group, 'Momentum Strategies Across Asset Classes' (2015)", "Bruder et al., 'Which Trend Is Your Friend?' (FAJ, 2013) \u2014 proves TSMOM and MA crossovers are mathematically equivalent"], "historical_sharpe": "0.78\u20131.0+ depending on universe and parameters (1972\u20132014); ML learning-to-rank variants show ~3x improvement over traditional momentum", "market_conditions": "Works in trending macro environments and periods of cross-asset dispersion. Fails during momentum crashes (sharp reversals after bear markets, e.g. March 2009), low-dispersion choppy regimes, and suffers from crowding/capacity constraints in liquid markets.", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "EFA", "GLD", "TLT", "AGG", "VTI"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "VIX + Yield Curve Regime-Based Rotation", "description": "Use VIX level/term structure and 10Y-2Y yield curve slope to classify market regime (risk-on, caution, risk-off). Shift asset allocation dynamically: equities in risk-on, Treasuries/gold in risk-off. VIX backwardation and yield curve inversion serve as early warning signals 2\u20134 weeks before drawdowns.", "evidence": ["Scientific Reports/Nature (2025), 'A Machine Learning Approach to Risk-Based Asset Allocation' \u2014 LSTM + risk budgeting, +187% vs. risk parity under high-VIX regimes", "arXiv:2406.09578 (2024), 'Dynamic Asset Allocation with Asset-Specific Regime Forecasts' \u2014 uses 2Y yield, 10Y-2Y slope, VIX as features", "arXiv:2410.14841 (2024), 'Dynamic Factor Allocation Leveraging Regime-Switching Signals'", "Hidden Markov Model study (2004\u20132026): Sharpe 2.66 within low-volatility regime; 73% of 10%+ equity drawdowns preceded by identifiable VIX term structure configurations"], "historical_sharpe": "1.22 overall (2004\u20132026 HMM study); 2.66 within low-volatility regimes; max drawdown -19.5% vs. -55.2% for SPY buy-and-hold", "market_conditions": "Strong in clear regime transitions (2008, COVID 2020). Major failure in 2022 when yield curve inversion AND high VIX signaled defensive shift to bonds, which then lost 15\u201320% simultaneously \u2014 both classic safe havens failed together.", "instruments": ["SPY", "TLT", "AGG", "GLD", "IWM"], "timeframe": "weekly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Hierarchical Risk Parity (HRP)", "description": "Portfolio construction method using hierarchical clustering on the correlation matrix followed by recursive bisection to allocate inverse-variance weights. Avoids inverting the covariance matrix (the key instability in mean-variance optimization), producing more stable out-of-sample weights. Best used as an allocation engine layered on top of a momentum signal.", "evidence": ["Marcos L\u00f3pez de Prado, 'Building Diversified Portfolios that Outperform Out of Sample', Journal of Portfolio Management (2016)", "PyPortfolioOpt HRPOpt implementation; riskfolio-lib", "NVIDIA Technical Blog: HRP on RAPIDS GPU implementation", "Comparative study: HRP reduces out-of-sample variance by 31% vs. classical mean-variance optimization (CLA)"], "historical_sharpe": "+31.3% Sharpe improvement vs. MVO out-of-sample; net Sharpe ~0.94\u20131.26 depending on turnover management; works best when combined with a momentum signal layer", "market_conditions": "Works best with large diverse universes (10+ assets) where correlation structure is informative. Fails with small universes (our 8-asset universe is borderline), during 2008-style crises where all correlations spike to 1, and suffers from high turnover eroding net returns without explicit constraints.", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "VTI", "GLD", "TLT", "AGG", "EFA"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Defensive Asset Allocation (DAA) \u2014 Keller's VAA Successor", "description": "Improvement on VAA using a separate 'canary universe' (EEM + AGG) as the breadth sensor instead of checking all offensive assets. If the canary signals stress, shift a fraction of capital defensive (not all-or-nothing). Reduces the false defensive signals triggered by AGG weakness alone, improving returns during equity bull markets while maintaining crash protection.", "evidence": ["Keller & Keuning, 'Breadth Momentum and the Canary Universe: Defensive Asset Allocation' (SSRN 3307823)", "Addresses the primary documented failure mode of VAA: AGG as both an offensive and defensive trigger caused premature exits"], "historical_sharpe": "Improved over VAA's 0.94; reduced false defensive signals; max drawdown similar to VAA at ~-13% to -15%", "market_conditions": "Inherits VAA's crash-protection strengths while reducing opportunity cost during bond bear markets. Still fails in fast whipsaw regimes and environments where EEM and AGG simultaneously signal stress without a real equity bear market following.", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "EFA", "GLD", "TLT", "AGG"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "low"}, {"name": "Intermarket Divergence Overlay (Gold-Bond-Equity)", "description": "Use divergence between gold (GLD) and long-duration Treasuries (TLT) as a regime filter. In normal risk-off regimes both rally together; divergence (one rallies, other doesn't confirm) signals a regime shift. Gold up + bonds flat/down = inflationary stress, reduce bond exposure. Both falling = rate shock environment, shift to cash/short duration (AGG/SHY). Used as an overlay on a primary trend strategy.", "evidence": ["John Murphy, Intermarket Technical Analysis (1991) and Intermarket Analysis (2004) \u2014 foundational practitioner framework", "17+ years of claimed out-of-sample robustness without re-optimization (EasyLanguageMastery)", "COVID March 2020: simultaneous Treasury yield collapse + USD surge gave early bottom signal", "Note: limited peer-reviewed academic validation; primarily practitioner/empirical evidence"], "historical_sharpe": "No standalone peer-reviewed Sharpe; best used as a 0/1 filter to improve Sharpe of a primary strategy by 0.1\u20130.2 by avoiding regime-break periods", "market_conditions": "Works during inflationary transitions, risk-off flights-to-safety, and dollar-driven cycles. Completely broke down in 2022 when both gold and bonds sold off simultaneously under inflation-driven rate hikes \u2014 the foundational correlation regime failed.", "instruments": ["GLD", "TLT", "AGG"], "timeframe": "weekly", "complexity": "low"}, {"name": "Tax-Loss Harvesting + January Effect Calendar Overlay", "description": "Exploit year-end tax-loss selling pressure: buy small-cap ETFs (IWM) in the last week of December when tax-motivated selling depresses prices, sell in the first week of January. As a harvesting strategy: systematically sell losing positions in December and reinvest in correlated-but-not-identical ETFs (e.g., swap VTI for SPY) to capture tax deductions while maintaining market exposure.", "evidence": ["Keim (1983) and Roll (1983) \u2014 original January Effect papers", "Brauer & Chang (1990), 'The Turn-of-the-Year Effect and Tax-Loss-Selling by Institutional Investors'", "Zheng (University of Michigan), 'Tax-Loss Selling and the January Effect' \u2014 mean equal-weighted January return 4.5% vs. 0.8% other months", "EFMA 2025, 'The January Effect Before Tax-Loss Selling' \u2014 effect predates income tax, confirming behavioral/institutional drivers beyond pure tax mechanics", "ScienceDirect (2022), 'Tax-Loss Harvesting under Uncertainty', Journal of Banking & Finance"], "historical_sharpe": "0.5\u20131.5% annual after-tax alpha from harvesting (not alpha generation); small-cap January anomaly: 5.3% mean January return vs. 1.7% other months (1960\u20132020), concentrated in first 5 trading days; effect has weakened ~40% as institutions dominate", "market_conditions": "Works in taxable accounts with realized gains to offset; small-cap, high-volatility, year-to-date losers benefit most. Weakening due to institutional dominance (85% of volume), rise of tax-advantaged accounts, and arbitrage by quants buying in late December.", "instruments": ["IWM", "VTI", "SPY"], "timeframe": "weekly", "complexity": "low"}], "recommendation": "Start with Keller's Defensive Asset Allocation (DAA) as the primary strategy, layered with the Intermarket Divergence Overlay as a regime filter. DAA improves on VAA's documented AGG-sensitivity flaw while preserving the core breadth-momentum crash protection that produced Sharpe ~1.0+ over 45+ years. It requires only monthly rebalancing to a single ETF, is implementable with $1,000, incurs minimal transaction costs (1\u20134 trades/year), and has a clear mechanical rule with no curve-fitting ambiguity. The intermarket gold-bond divergence overlay adds a low-cost regime sanity check \u2014 when GLD and TLT diverge sharply, treat any 'risk-on' signal from DAA with increased skepticism and reduce position sizing. For the given instrument set (SPY, QQQ, IWM, VTI, GLD, TLT, AGG, EFA), a practical DAA implementation would use SPY/QQQ/IWM/EFA as the offensive universe, EEM+AGG as the canary, and TLT/AGG as the defensive allocation. If backtesting reveals the 2022 simultaneous bond+equity drawdown is unacceptable, add GLD as a third defensive option alongside TLT \u2014 gold's lack of correlation to rate moves provides the missing safe haven that bonds no longer reliably offer in inflationary regimes."}
